title: 11 Tir — Timqat (The Feast of Epiphany) & The Assembly
narrative:
The eleventh day of Tir is the feast of Timqat — the Ethiopian celebration of the Epiphany, the Theophany, the Baptism of the Lord in the Jordan River — and it is the theological summit of the month, the moment toward which all the days of preparation have been reaching and from which all the days of aftermath will radiate. At dawn, after the night of vigil, the priest descends to the water and performs the great blessing — the sanctification of the waters in the name of the Holy Trinity. As the prayer of hallowing is chanted over the river or pool or lake, the faithful understand that they are not merely commemorating an event of thirty years after the Nativity but participating in it: the same Spirit who descended as a dove upon the waters of the Jordan is present and active above these waters, and their blessing is the same blessing that the Lord's descent imparted to all water for all time. The faithful plunge themselves into the blessed water, or receive it poured upon their heads, renewing the grace of their own baptisms, and the air is filled with the sound of the ancient Epiphany hymn: 'The Jordan turned back and the mountains skipped like rams.'

At the heart of the Timqat mystery lies the revelation of the Holy Trinity in its full and simultaneous disclosure: the Son standing in the water, the Spirit descending as a dove upon Him, the Father speaking from the opened heaven — 'This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.' This is the feast that Basil the Great called the beginning of the Church's theological knowledge of God; it is the feast that Gregory the Theologian called the illumination of the world; it is the feast that the Ethiopian tradition calls the renewal of all creation, for when the Spirit who moved over the waters at the first creation moves again over the waters of the Jordan, the new creation is inaugurated. The opening of the heavens above the Jordan is the reversal of the closing of the heavens at the fall of Adam: what was shut at Eden is opened at the Jordan, and the divine voice that had fallen silent since the last of the prophets speaks again with absolute clarity and authority.

Archangel Michael, whose monthly feast falls on this supreme day, stands as the captain of the angelic host that witnessed the Baptism of the Lord. The ancient texts of the liturgy describe the Baptism as attended by the entire hierarchy of heaven, the angels trembling at the sight of their Creator submitting to the water of His own creature. Michael, the archangel who challenged Lucifer's claim to equality with God with the cry 'Who is like God?', now witnesses the supreme answer to that question: the one who is like God — because He is God — has descended into the water of human mortality as the servant of the covenant, and in doing so has displayed a divine condescension more astonishing than any angelic imagination had conceived. Saint Theodosius the Cenobiarch, the great Palestinian monastic father of the fifth century who founded the community of common life that became the model of cenobitic monasticism in the Holy Land, is also commemorated on this day. Theodosius — whose name means 'given by God' — established his monastery in the wilderness between Jerusalem and the Dead Sea, within sight of the Jordan valley, as if the entire orientation of his community of prayer was toward the river where the Lord had been baptized.

The theological seal of this eleventh day of Tir — the day of Timqat — is the mystery of the Hallowing of the Waters and the Voice of the Father. When the Father speaks from the opened heaven, He speaks a word that has never been spoken before in human history: He names His Son publicly, before all creation, in the language of love — 'beloved' — and of divine pleasure. This word is not merely information; it is itself a creative act. As the divine word at the first creation brought light out of darkness, so this word at the new creation brings the new humanity out of the old. Every soul that has ever been baptized has had this word spoken over it in secret; Timqat is the annual moment when the Church hears that word spoken aloud again over the water, remembers that she herself is beloved, remembers that the Father is well pleased with all who are hidden in His Son — and plunges, weeping and rejoicing, into the blessed river of her own rebirth.
